skill.name: uuid
skill.description: Guides UUID v4 and v7 generation for TypeScript, Angular, Firestore, and event-driven entity identifiers.
skill.summary: Generate and validate UUID v4 and v7 identifiers for TypeScript application data.
skill.author: 7Spade

security_audit.summary: Static findings were mostly false positives caused by markdown code fences, TypeScript examples, and a repository URL. No prompt injection, malware intent, credential access, or data exfiltration was found, but a session example uses a UUID as a token before later warning against that pattern.

medium_findings.title: UUID Used as a Session Token in Example Code
medium_findings.description: The session management example creates a token with uuidv4(), which can encourage use of UUIDs as authentication tokens. The later anti-pattern section warns against this, so the issue appears to be unsafe guidance rather than malicious intent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly assigns generateToken() to a session token and generateToken() returns uuidv4(). The same file later identifies client-side UUID tokens as an anti-pattern, confirming the security relevance.

low_findings.title: Markdown and TypeScript Examples Misidentified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: The external command findings primarily point to markdown code fences and TypeScript examples, not executable Ruby or shell backtick code. The CLI section does show user-run npx uuid examples, but they are transparent documentation for the uuid package and do not execute automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review shows the flagged backticks are markdown fences or TypeScript template literals. The only shell content is a visible CLI usage example, not hidden execution logic.

low_findings.title: Repository URL Misclassified as Network Behavior
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL is package metadata pointing to the uuid project repository. No HTTP client, fetch call, webhook, or runtime network behavior is present in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is in the Package Information section and contains only a repository reference. I found no evidence of code that sends or retrieves data over the network.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts refer to UUID version text, field names, or performance notes, not MD5, SHA-1, or custom cryptography. The reconnaissance alerts point to ordinary identifier, progress, and command examples without host inspection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no system probing commands, filesystem discovery, or weak hash implementation. The suspicious words are part of UUID documentation and application examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authentication Token Example Uses uuidv4
dangerous_patterns.description: Using UUIDs as session tokens can weaken authentication design and contradicts the later recommendation to use a dedicated authentication token provider. This should be revised before broad publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code path clearly returns uuidv4() for generateToken(), and the anti-pattern section confirms that this is a discouraged security practice.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Run npx Commands Documented
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents npx uuid commands. They are not executed by the skill, but marketplace users should understand that npx may download and run package code in their environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are explicit and limited to uuid generation examples. The remaining risk is normal package execution risk, not evidence of hidden or malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Distributed apps need stable identifiers that do not collide. This skill guides UUID v4 and v7 use in TypeScript, Angular, Firestore, and event workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use UUID v4 for general entity identifiers.
Explains when to use UUID v7 for sortable time-based records.
Shows TypeScript and Angular repository patterns using the uuid package.
Covers UUID validation and version checks with library helpers.
Documents Firestore storage guidance and identifier anti-patterns.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not install packages or run commands automatically.
It focuses on JavaScript and TypeScript examples, especially Angular and Firestore.
It should not be used as guidance for authentication token generation.
It does not provide database migration scripts for existing identifier schemes.

use_cases.title: Add Entity Identifiers
use_cases.description: Choose UUID v4 for tasks, users, uploads, and other records that need unique IDs across services.

use_cases.title: Design Sortable Event IDs
use_cases.description: Use UUID v7 for audit logs, event streams, and time-series records that benefit from chronological ordering.

use_cases.title: Standardize Firestore IDs
use_cases.description: Apply consistent string UUID storage, validation, and typing patterns in Angular Firestore repositories.

prompt_templates.title: Pick a UUID Version
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose UUID v4 or UUID v7 for these records: [describe records]. Explain the tradeoffs in simple terms.

prompt_templates.title: Create TypeScript ID Helpers
prompt_templates.prompt: Create TypeScript helper functions for generating and validating UUIDs for [entity names]. Use the uuid package patterns from this skill.

prompt_templates.title: Review Repository ID Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Angular Firestore repository for UUID usage. Identify version choice, validation gaps, and storage concerns: [paste code].

prompt_templates.title: Plan an ID Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a migration from sequential IDs to UUIDs for this system: [describe schema and API]. Include compatibility, indexing, and rollout risks.

output_examples.input: I need IDs for audit log entries that sort by creation time.
output_examples.output: Use UUID v7 because it includes timestamp ordering. Store it as a string and keep the normal createdAt field for queries.

output_examples.input: I need random IDs for uploaded files in a distributed app.
output_examples.output: Use UUID v4 for upload records. Validate incoming IDs and avoid deriving access rights from the identifier alone.

output_examples.input: Can I use UUIDs for login session tokens?
output_examples.output: Use a dedicated authentication token provider instead. UUIDs are identifiers, not a complete session security design.

best_practices: Use UUID v4 for general entities and UUID v7 for records that need chronological sorting.
Validate UUIDs at API and repository boundaries before reading or writing records.
Store UUIDs as strings in Firestore unless a measured storage need requires another format.
anti_patterns: Do not use sequential counters for distributed entity identifiers.
Do not manually parse UUID substrings when library helpers are available.
Do not use UUID generation as a substitute for secure authentication token management.

faq.question: What UUID versions does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers UUID v4 for random identifiers and UUID v7 for time-ordered identifiers.

faq.question: Does the skill run any commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It only documents examples, including optional npx commands that a user may run manually.

faq.question: Can I use UUID v7 in Firestore document IDs?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill recommends storing UUIDs as strings, which works with Firestore document identifiers.

faq.question: Are UUIDs safe for session tokens?
faq.answer: Do not rely on UUIDs for session security. Use a dedicated authentication provider or token service.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is this skill specific to Angular?
faq.answer: The examples emphasize Angular and Firestore, but the UUID guidance applies to TypeScript applications in general.
